title: The Ukrainian family torn by war and exile behind a famous photo published by Le Monde
subhead: A few days after the Russian invasion, photojournalist Laurence Geai captured the farewell of a Ukrainian family at Odesa train station. Four years later, the mother welcomed Le Monde to Cologne, Germany, where she lives as a refugee with her son and her mother, far from her husband, since enlisted in the army.

France summons US ambassador over comments on slain far-right activist
The Syrian oil sector's year zero
subhead: By driving the Kurds out of northeastern Syria, the central government has regained control over all of the country's oil resources. However, given the poor condition of the wells, damaged by fighting and chaotic extraction, it will take several years before production returns to its pre-war level.

title: Oleksandr Syrsky, commander of the Ukrainian army, to Le Monde: 'Russian army losses have exceeded its recruitment level'
subhead: The Ukrainian commander-in-chief told Le Monde that, despite a 'very difficult' situation on the battlefield, his army continued to resist 'the most powerful army in Europe' and to 'foil' Russian military plans.

title: 2026 Winter Olympics: The most striking photos from the Games
subhead: The 2026 edition of the Winter Olympic Games in Milan-Cortina, Italy, was filled with outstanding performances.

title: AI has arrived in France's local elections – for better or worse
subhead: From creating campaign videos to writing speeches, artificial intelligence has become an effective tool for candidates. However, it also raises ethical questions, prompting some elected officials to call for regulation.

US military buildup around Iran: What satellite images show
subhead: Amid negotiations over Iran's nuclear program, Donald Trump amasses US military assets at bases and in the surrounding seas. Using satellite imagery and other open sources, Le Monde documented an unprecedented deployment.
byline: Brice Donadille,  Fabien Leboucq and  Liselotte Mas

title: Iran expert Ross Harrison: 'Cutting off the head of the snake' would not bring down the regime
subhead: Ross Harrison, a nonresident scholar at the Middle East Institute and an expert on the Islamic Republic, sees a growing likelihood of a US-led intervention aimed at causing the regime to implode. However, he believes the chances of success are limited, given the particularly 'resilient' nature of the Iranian system, as he explains in an interview with Le Monde.

title: How a messianic movement is changing the Israeli military
subhead: Followers of the Hardal movement, a blend of religious fundamentalism and ultranationalism, have begun to make inroads into the military establishment. Their dehumanizing rhetoric toward Palestinians has contributed to the destruction of the Gaza Strip.
